data.title: Activist investor Ancora pitches Amcor with its planned acquisition of Berry
data.description:
Activist investor Amcor (NYSE:AMCR) pitched Amcor at an investor conference on the merits of its planned $8.4 billion all-stock takeover of Berry Global (BERY) that will combine the two packaging companies.

Ancora believes Amcor (AMCR) may have 60% upside to $16 a share from its takeover of Berry (BERY), Ancora President Jim Chadwick said at the Bloomberg Activism Forum in New York City on Tuesday.

Chadwick sees limited to zero U.S. antitrust issues with a potential deal, as the businesses are highly complimentary.

Packaging solutions company Amcor (AMCR) agreed to acquire peer Berry (BERY) in an $8.4B all-stock transaction last month.

data.title: Barrick Gold said to seek buyer for Ivory Coast mine- report
data.description:
Barrick Gold (NYSE:GOLD) is looking for buyers for its Tongon gold mine in the Ivory Coast, Bloomberg News reported on Tuesday, citing people familiar with the matter.

The gold miner started working with Toronto-Dominion Bank to identify buyers and solicit bids for the mine in late November, according to the report.

Barrick (GOLD) plans to stop production at the 24-year-old Tongon mine by 2027. The mine produced 204,000 ounces last year, which was about 5% of the company’s overall gold output for the year.

Barrick has about 90% stake in Tongon, with the Ivory Coast government and local investors holding the rest of the operation.

Barrick did not immediately respond to a Seeking Alpha request for comment on the report.

Barrick Gold (GOLD) shares were up more than 1% in afternoon trading.

data.title: U.S. small business optimism jumps to highest level in over three years on Trump boost
data.description:
A key monthly measure of optimism among U.S. small business owners on Tuesday surged to its highest level since June 2021, primarily driven by Donald Trump's election victory.

The NFIB small business optimism index rose by eight points in November to 101.7, after 34 months of remaining below the 50-year average of 98.

Small businesses form a big bulk of the U.S. economy. The Small Business Administration (SBA) is responsible for helping entrepreneurs and small businesses get the resources needed to start or expand their profession or to recover from disasters.

According to the SBA, the agency in FY 2025 will support at least $43B in 7(a), 504 and micro small business loans, over $7B in bonding (including both bids and final bonds), and over $45B in overall investment capitalization to startups and growth-oriented firms.

“The election results signal a major shift in economic policy, leading to a surge in optimism among small business owners,” NFIB chief economist Bill Dunkelberg said in a statement on Tuesday.

“Main Street also became more certain about future business conditions following the election, breaking a nearly three-year streak of record high uncertainty. Owners are particularly hopeful for tax and regulation policies that favor strong economic growth as well as relief from inflationary pressures. In addition, small business owners are eager to expand their operations,” Dunkelberg added.

The state of the economy was found to be the top concern among voters in the U.S. presidential election. Since Trump's victory, Wall Street has scaled fresh record highs amid an expectation that the former President's second term will once again pursue tax-cut, tariff-based policies and will attempt to stimulate economic growth.

In Tuesday's data from the NFIB, the net percent of small business owners expecting the economy to improve rose 41 points in November from October to a net 36%, the highest since June 2020. This component had the greatest impact on the overall increase in the optimism index.

Additionally, the NFIB found that twenty percent of owners in November indicated inflation as their single most important problem in operating their business (higher input and labor costs), down three points from October and surpassing labor quality as the top issue by one point.

The NFIB has collected Small Business economic trends data with quarterly surveys since the fourth quarter of 1973 and monthly surveys since 1986.

For investors looking to track the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) index, here are some exchange-traded funds of interest: (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

data.title: Capital One sees closing Discover deal early 2025, adding 'rare and valuable' asset
data.description:
Discover Financial Service's (DFS) card network is a "rare and valuable asset," Capital One Financial (NYSE:COF) Chairman and CEO Richard Fairbank said Tuesday at an industry conference.

It's a piece that connects the credit card issuer with merchants, he noted. The acquisition is a "unique opportunity" for Capital One (COF) to bring the scale that the Discover network needs.

Capital One (COF) remains enthusiastic about the pending acquisition of Discover (DFS). "Everything that we can see, we continue to be very excited," Fairbank said. "What we expected going in is still pretty consistent with what we're seeing."

Fairbank is expecting the Discover (DFS) shareholder vote on the deal will occur early next in 2025. Capital One (COF) has submitted everything needed by the banking regulators. The process with the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency and the Federal Reserve, its two banking regulators, continues to move along. The company is also continuing to engage with the Federal Trade Commission.

SEC has asked Discover to file a restatement of financials relative to issues on the network pricing side. While that's creating a delay, it hasn't affected the earnings.

The deal's economics are still within the range Capital One expected when it agreed to the acquisition, said Jeff Norris, senior vice president of finance at the company.

The company expects to be in a strong excess capital after the acquisition closes, he noted. It's likely to lead to increased distributions to shareholders, Norris said, without being specific.

Capital One (COF) stock edged down 0.3% in afternoon trading.

Looking toward the broader economy, "the consumer is a source of strength in the economy these days, and has been for quite some time," Fairbank said. Wages are finally increasing. The savings rate is still pretty healthy, assisted by a recent revision in the saving number.

"I think the consumer is in quite a good place," he said.

Charge-offs are a bit higher for Capital One (COF) and for the industry than pre-pandemic. "Credit is very much settling out," from the unusually low levels during the pandemic.

The reason for that is a "mathematical" phenomenon, as recoveries of charged-off debt are running much lower than prepandemic levels. Inventory amounts have been lower, leading to lower recoveries, he explained.

He also cited a delayed charge-off effect of the pandemic. During the crisis, there was unprecedented government stimulus, resulting in a massive surplus that came to consumers. That may have tided over consumers for a while, but their underlying situation didn't improve.

"Credit losses play out by things that are happening at the margins."

Capital One (COF) is "running at notably higher payment rates than prepandemic, but percentage of people paying a lower portion of their balance is also increasing," Fairbank said. "That may explain why elevated charge-offs are playing out."

Capital One's (COF) purchase volume vs. prepandemic is about 45% higher. However, much of that is the company's growth, he observed. Spend per consumer is basically flat. Weakness in purchase volume was coming in at the lower end of the market.

Overall, "things are pretty stable" for the average consumer on the purchase volume side, he said.

data.title: S&P 500 wobbles as Oracle slides, Alphabet jumps; Nasdaq hits record intraday high
data.description:
Major U.S. stock averages sought firm direction Tuesday afternoon, with Big Tech back in the spotlight as communication services stocks climbed while Oracle (ORCL) suffered following its quarterly update.

Stocks were coming off a negative session on Monday, after notching a three-week win streak. A cautious tone has been setting in ahead of consumer and wholesale inflation reports due this week.

The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) -0.1% after scaling a fresh intraday record high of 19,887.08 points. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) -0.1% after notching small gains. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was flat in afternoon trade.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was up 3 basis points to 4.23%, and the 2-year yield (US2Y) was also up 3 basis points to 4.16%.

The S&P 500's (SP500) Communication Services sector +2.4%, pacing gains in five other sectors, bolstered by Alphabet. Class A (GOOGL) and class C (GOOG) shares of the tech titan rose +5%, moving up a day after the company revealed a powerful quantum computing chip.

But Oracle (ORCL) on the index sank -7.7% Tuesday after the cloud software company disappointed investors with fiscal second-quarter results that slightly missed expectations.

Traders will receive the consumer price inflation for November on Wednesday, ahead of next week's Federal Reserve monetary policy committee meeting, the last of the year. The core consumer price index is expected to come in at 0.3% M/M, and 3.3% Y/Y.

The headline number is forecast to come in at 0.2% on a monthly basis, and 2.7% annually.

"That release (CPI) is going to be a crucial one, as it’s probably the last big piece of the jigsaw ahead of the Fed’s policy decision next week," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid said.

In other stock movers, developer data platform MongoDB (MDB) -15% despite delivering a top- and bottom-line beat and lifting its annual revenue and adjusted profit guidance. Michael Gordon, MongoDB's CFO and COO for nearly a decade, will retire in January.

In Tuesday's economic calendar, Q3 labor costs were revised down, while the productivity number stayed at 2.2%.

data.title: Apple to bring satellite connectivity, other changes, to Apple Watch next year: report
data.description:
Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) is planning to make several changes to several models of the Apple Watch next year, including satellite connectivity, Bloomberg reported.

Shares of the tech giant rose fractionally in midday trading, while Globalstar (NYSE:GSAT), its satellite connectivity partner, rose 8.8%.

The new connectivity feature is set to come to the high-end version of the watch, the Apple Watch Ultra, the news outlet added, citing people familiar with the matter. The feature, first introduced as part of the iPhone 14 lineup in 2022, allows people to send messages and calls when they do not have Wi-Fi or cellular connectivity. The emergency service was included free for two years, but so far, Apple has not charged for the service.

Last month, Globalstar shares jumped after it announced an expanded deal with Apple (AAPL) that saw Apple make an infrastructure prepayment of up to $1.1B and take a 20% stake in the company.

In addition, Apple is increasing its focus on a blood-pressure feature for the Watch and could arrive as soon as next year, Bloomberg added. Apple has worked on the feature for some time now, though it has been marred by delays.

The Apple Watch may also get an internal change, with some models having modems built by MediaTek, replacing those made by Intel (NASDAQ:INTC), Bloomberg added. Apple is working on in-house modems for the iPhone in an effort to rely less on external partners and control more of its devices.

Intel shares fell 3% in late afternoon trading.

Apple and Intel did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Phillips 66 partners with NextEra to power Rodeo refinery with solar energy
data.description:
Phillips 66 (NYSE:PSX) said Tuesday it will power its Rodeo refinery in California with a new 30.2 MW solar facility owned and operated by NextEra Energy (NEE) (NEP), making it one of the largest on-site dedicated solar facilities in the U.S.; financial details were not disclosed.

The solar plant will be built on 88 acres of land owned by Phillips 66 (PSX), adjacent to its renewable fuels facility in the San Francisco Bay Area, and will consist of more than 70K solar modules that will support the renewable fuels complex.

The facility, which is designed to generate ~60K MWh/year of electricity, is expected to reduce the refinery's grid power demand by 50% and lower its carbon dioxide emissions by 33K metric tons/year starting in Q1 2025, Phillips 66 (PSX) said.

The company converted the refinery to producing renewable diesel from fats, vegetable oils and greases, after previously making gasoline and diesel from crude oil; the complex began processing only renewable feedstocks in March, achieving full processing capacity of 50K bbl/day in June.
